design_session_summary.date: Year 13,003 of the First Reckoning (project inception)
design_session_summary.status: World foundation established. Character creation system designed. Six starting regions detailed. History written. Systems architected.
what_is_locked: Currency system (Iron Chit / Bronze Mark / Steel Disc)
Religion system (5 faith families, 15+ specific faiths)

next_design_steps.description: The ~15 formal background types that the intake resolves into. Each with specific named skill bonuses, starting traits, starting inventory, and social standing. Examples: Levy Soldier, Tanner's Apprentice, Camp Follower, Dispossessed Farmhand, Disgraced Man-at-Arms, Urban Laborer, Temple Servant, Merchant's Clerk, Rural Midwife, Sailor, Criminal, Minor Noble (impoverished), etc.

Exact progression mechanics. How quickly skills advance by use. Diminishing returns curve. Instruction bonuses. Study bonuses (literacy required). Skill interaction rules (Strength affects One-Handed Blade ceiling, etc.).
Each starting region needs specific settlement maps, named NPCs, specific business instances, faction presence details, and concrete 'day one' scenarios.
All 42 powers with consistent detail: leadership, military strength, economic base, relationships with neighbors, current Year 13,003 condition.
Turn-based or real-time? Wound locations and effects. Weapon vs. armor interaction. Morale in combat. Surrender and capture mechanics. Fleeing mechanics.

Detailed design of how players progress toward large goals: becoming a mercenary captain, rising to lordship, building a trade empire, becoming a guild master, etc. These are earned/taken, never selected.
How faith affiliation actually affects gameplay. Temple access. Community support. Faction disposition. Pilgrim routes. Religious conflict zones.
Complete procedural NPC generation tables. Name combination rules. Profession distribution by settlement type. Relationship generation. The 'secret or complication' table.

What exactly was found in the Ossrath ruins. What is on the ship in the Anchorage. What is causing the contamination in the Free Fens. How these threads connect. How they can be found, ignored, or resolved by players over long playthroughs.
Actual cartography. Continent sizes, relative positions, ocean routes, major road networks, no-man's-land zones, resource distribution.

technical_design_notes.rendering: To be determined. Options: ASCII/Unicode on Canvas (classic roguelike), Colored tiles (no sprites), Tilesets (more visual, more work). Colored ASCII is recommended starting point.
technical_design_notes.game_loop: Turn-based at player action level. World simulation runs on its own clock.
technical_design_notes.architecture: Game state in plain objects/arrays (serialization trivial). Logic completely separate from rendering.
technical_design_notes.prototype_approach: Start with one room and one NPC. Get the full loop working before building the world simulation.
technical_design_notes.scope_note: The full vision described here is an enormously ambitious project — years of work. A working prototype should target a single starting region with simplified simulation systems, then expand.
technical_design_notes.recommended_first_build: Thornsveld Marches, single town (Durnward), 20-30 named NPCs, basic economic simulation (food, coin, one trade good), player needs system, basic combat.

thematic_reminders: The world does not care if the player lives or dies
Death should feel mundane, not dramatic
No source of anything is infinite
The history is in the landscape — layers of time accumulate
The Homeworld/EVE loneliness comes from scale and indifference, not from emptiness
Your goal is your win — there is no imposed victory condition
Small things matter — a dependent somewhere, an old debt, a name that means something in one town
The world is recovering from the last apocalypse while slowly building toward the next one
